session_title: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
session_description: A Main Stage panel at the Munich Security Conference 2026 examining whether the global order has reached a breaking point. The panelists debate whether the world is now defined by might and spheres of influence rather than rules; the costs and risks this creates even for great and middle powers; how defenders of multilateralism and international law should communicate its benefits; where reform of existing international institutions remains promising versus where coalitions of the willing should take over; and how middle and rising powers can leverage their position in a might-makes-right world to keep multilateralism alive.
venue: Munich Security Conference 2026, Main Stage I

roster.name: Zanny Minton Beddoes
roster.title: Editor-in-Chief (Moderator)
roster.bio: British journalist and editor-in-chief of The Economist since 2015, the first woman to hold the role in the publication's 180-year history. Economist by training, with prior experience at the IMF working on the transition economies of Eastern Europe and Russia. Frequently moderates high-profile international affairs panels and is a familiar presence at Davos and Munich. Known for crisp, well-prepped questioning that pushes panelists toward specifics rather than letting them retreat into talking points.

roster.name: Kaja Kallas
roster.title: Vice-President of the European Commission and High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy
roster.affiliation: European Commission; former Prime Minister of Estonia
roster.bio: European Union's chief diplomat since taking office in late 2024. Former Prime Minister of Estonia (2021–2024), where she became one of Europe's most outspoken voices in support of Ukraine after Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022. Daughter of Siim Kallas, a former Estonian PM and EU commissioner. Frames Russian aggression as an existential threat to the European order and consistently argues for harder sanctions, more weapons to Ukraine, and stronger EU defense capabilities. Member of the MSC Advisory Council.

roster.name: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
roster.title: Minister of Foreign Affairs
roster.bio: Saudi Arabia's Foreign Minister since 2019. Career diplomat and member of the Saudi royal family, previously ambassador to Germany. Has overseen Saudi Arabia's diplomatic repositioning under Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, including the China-brokered rapprochement with Iran in 2023, ongoing efforts toward Israeli-Saudi normalization, and Saudi Arabia's increasingly independent posture between Washington and Beijing. Regular voice at MSC speaking for the Gulf perspective on regional security, Israel-Palestine, and the broader question of how middle powers should navigate a multipolar world.

roster.name: Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez
roster.title: Major General; Minister of National Defence
roster.bio: Colombian Major General and Minister of National Defence under President Gustavo Petro's left-wing government. Career military officer who took the civilian defence role during a period of major shifts in Colombian security policy, including the 'Total Peace' negotiations with armed groups, ongoing tensions with Venezuela and Ecuador, and Colombia's evolving relationship with the United States on counter-narcotics. Speaks from a Latin American perspective on global security questions, with particular attention to how middle powers in the Global South can defend international law without aligning with either Washington or Beijing.

roster.name: Michael Waltz
roster.title: Ambassador of the United States of America to the United Nations
roster.bio: U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations under the second Trump administration. Former U.S. Representative for Florida's 6th congressional district (2019–2025) and former National Security Advisor to President Trump (early 2025), a role he left under controversy. Retired Green Beret and Special Forces veteran with combat experience in Afghanistan and the Middle East. Hawkish on China, supports a tougher stance against Iran and Venezuela, has been more skeptical of long-term Ukraine aid than the European panelists. Represents the Trump administration's view of international institutions as instruments to be used selectively rather than defended on principle.
